corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

top_candidates.sentence: State-of-the-art methods often learn a (complex) regression function that maps sentence embeddings to a similarity score.
Even though transfer learning is not the purpose of SBERT, it outperforms other state-of-the-art sentence embeddings methods on this task.
Even though transfer learning is not the purpose of SBERT, it outperforms other state-of-the-art sentence embeddings methods on this task.
However, for Sent-Eval, average BERT embeddings and the BERT CLS-token output achieves decent results (Table 5), outperforming average GloVe embeddings.
However, using the described fine-tuning setup with a siamese network structure on NLI datasets yields sentence embeddings that achieve a new state-of-the-art for the SentEval toolkit.
The logistic regression classifier is trained on various tasks in a 10-fold cross-validation setup and the prediction accuracy is computed for the test-fold.

We compare the SBERT sentence embeddings to other sentence embeddings methods on the following seven SentEval transfer tasks: • MR: Sentiment prediction for movie reviews snippets on a five start scale . • CR: Sentiment prediction of customer product reviews . • SUBJ: Subjectivity prediction of sentences from movie reviews and plot summaries . • MPQA: Phrase level opinion polarity classification from newswire . • SST: Stanford Sentiment Treebank with binary labels . • TREC: Fine grained question-type classification from TREC . • MRPC: Microsoft Research Paraphrase Corpus from parallel news sources .
The only dataset where SBERT is significantly worse than Universal Sentence Encoder is the TREC dataset.
Universal Sentence Encoder was pre-trained on question-answering data, which appears to be beneficial for the question-type classification task of the TREC dataset.
For the classification objective function, we train SBERTbase on the SNLI and the Multi-NLI dataset.
For the regression objective function, we train on the training set of the STS benchmark dataset.
For the regression objective function, we train on the training set of the STS benchmark dataset.
Performances are measured on the development split of the STS benchmark dataset.
Performances are measured on the development split of the STS benchmark dataset.

NLI STSb Pooling Strategy MEAN 80.78 87.44 MAX 79.07 69.92 CLS 79.80 86.62 Concatenation (u, v) 66.04 -(|u -v|) 69.78 -(u * v) 70.54 -(|u -v|, u * v) 78.37 -(u, v, u * v) 77.44 -(u, v, |u -v|) 80.78 -(u, v, |u -v|, u * v) 80.44 -Table 6: SBERT trained on NLI data with the classification objective function, on the STS benchmark (STSb) with the regression objective function.
Configurations are evaluated on the development set of the STSb using cosine-similarity and Spearman's rank correlation.
At inference, when predicting similarities for the STS benchmark dataset, only the sentence embeddings u and v are used in combination with cosine-similarity.
At inference, when predicting similarities for the STS benchmark dataset, only the sentence embeddings u and v are used in combination with cosine-similarity.
The objective function (classification vs. regression) depends on the annotated dataset.
BERT and RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019) has set a new state-of-the-art performance on sentence-pair regression tasks like semantic textual similarity (STS).

In this publication, we present Sentence-BERT (SBERT), a modification of the pretrained BERT network that use siamese and triplet network structures to derive semantically meaningful sentence embeddings that can be compared using cosine-similarity.
This reduces the effort for finding the most similar pair from 65 hours with BERT / RoBERTa to about 5 seconds with SBERT, while maintaining the accuracy from BERT.
We evaluate SBERT and SRoBERTa on common STS tasks and transfer learning tasks, where it outperforms other state-of-the-art sentence embeddings methods.
We evaluate SBERT and SRoBERTa on common STS tasks and transfer learning tasks, where it outperforms other state-of-the-art sentence embeddings methods.
In this publication, we present Sentence-BERT (SBERT), a modification of the BERT network using siamese and triplet networks that is able to derive semantically meaningful sentence embeddings .
